description: Figure 1 presents reverse bending fatigue S-N curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy B specimens oriented longitudinally (BL, solid line with filled triangles ▲) and transversely (BT, dashed line with open triangles △). The plot shows applied stress amplitude (MPa) versus cycles to failure on a logarithmic abscissa spanning 10^5 to 2×10^7 cycles. Alloy B contains 10% ferrite and 90% austenite after solution treatment. The BL curve lies consistently above the BT curve across the entire tested range, indicating that the longitudinal specimen possesses superior fatigue resistance compared with the transverse specimen. This orientation dependence is attributed to the string arrangement of the ferrite phase: when the ferrite strings are perpendicular to the crack propagation direction (BL), the soft ferrite grains impede crack advance via a blunting effect and intense work hardening, acting as crack arresters; when parallel (BT), fatigue cracks propagate across austenite grains with less impediment. The figure thus experimentally demonstrates that ferrite phase orientation relative to the applied stress governs the fatigue performance in this duplex alloy system.

Optical micrograph showing fatigue crack propagation in specimen BT (transverse orientation) of alloy B, a duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy containing 10% ferrite and 90% austenite after solution treatment at 1050°C for 1.5 h followed by oil quenching. The micrograph, captured with a 50 μm scale bar, reveals a fatigue crack traversing directly across austenite grains. The ferrite phase appears as a string-like or banded arrangement aligned roughly parallel to the crack propagation direction. The caption states that in specimen BT the fatigue cracks propagated across the austenite grains. This contrasts with specimen BL (longitudinal orientation), where perpendicular ferrite bands impede crack growth. The image supports the core conclusion that when ferrite stringers are parallel to the crack path, the crack propagates unimpeded through the austenite matrix, whereas perpendicular ferrite bands act as crack arresters via blunting and work hardening, explaining the inferior fatigue resistance of BT relative to BL.

This optical micrograph displays the fatigue crack propagation behavior in specimen BL of an Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy (Fe-29Mn-9.1Al-0.6C) after solution treatment at 1050°C for 1.5 h and oil quenching. The microstructure consists of approximately 10% ferrite in a string-like arrangement embedded in 90% austenite matrix. The image captures multiple fatigue cracks that initiated in the austenite phase and propagated roughly perpendicular to the applied stress direction. In specimen BL, where the ferrite string arrangement is oriented perpendicular to the crack propagation direction, the advancing cracks are visibly impeded, blunted, or deflected at the ferrite bands. The bright stringers represent the soft ferrite phase, while the darker matrix is austenite with visible slip bands. The scale bar indicates 50 μm. This micrograph directly supports the conclusion that the ferrite phase acts as a crack arrester, explaining why the longitudinal specimen BL exhibits superior fatigue resistance compared to the transverse specimen BT, where ferrite strings align parallel to the crack path.
